7.title: Sequence to Sequence Generation
7.body: with Transformer Based Models Input: Sequence x1,., xT Output: Sequence y1,., yT Transformer is an encoder-decoder architecture: An encoder (neural network) analyzes the input and builds an intermediate representation (.context vector) of it; decoder (neural network) analyzes the intermediate representation and creates an output. Source: https://huggingface.co/blog/encoder-decoder

8.title: Transformer Based Models
8.body: Transformer is an encoder-decoder architecture with core component attention mechanism. Attention allows the model to focus on the most relevant parts of the input sequence when generating the output. Source: https://huggingface.co/blog/encoder-decoder

9.title: Example:
9.body: Language Translation with Transformers Source: https://huggingface.co/blog/encoder-decoder Transformers utilize an autoregressive approach for output generation.

10.title: Example:
10.body: Language Translation with Transformers Source: https://huggingface.co/blog/encoder-decoder Autoregressive generation is the inference-time procedure of iteratively calling model with its own generated outputs, given few initial inputs.

17.title: Positional Encoding
17.body: Source: Attention is all you need, NeurIPS, 2017. Desiderata of pos(.): 1. It should output unique encoding for each time-step (word position in sentence) 2. Distance between any two time-steps should be consistent across sentences with different lengths. 3. Our model should generalize to longer sentences without any efforts. Its values should be bounded. 4. It must be deterministic.

25.title: Self-Attention Layer
25.body: Source: https://cs231n.stanford.edu/slides/2022/lecture_11_ruohan.pdf Permutation equivariant Self-attention layer doesn care about the orders of the inputs! Problem: How can we encode ordered sequences like language or spatially ordered image features? That why we need positional encoding!

31.title: Masked Self-Attention Layer
31.body: Source: Attention is All You Need, NIPS. 2017. Masked self-attention is used to ensure that the model doesn attend to some of the tokens in the input sequence during training or generation. The architecture of Vision Transformer (ViT)

32.title: Masked Self-Attention Layer
32.body: Source: Attention is All You Need, NIPS. 2017. Prevent vectors from looking at future vectors Future alignment scores are set to-infinity

33.title: The Transformer Decoder Block (4)
33.body: Multi-head attention block is NOT self-attention. It attends over encoder outputs. For image captions, this is how we inject image features into the decoder.

35.title: Text to Image Generation
35.body: with Diffusion Based Models Source: Hierarchical Text-Conditional Image Generation with CLIP Latents, arXiv, 2022. Text to Image Generation Generating realistic images from text descriptions by OpenAI DALL 2

36.title: Text to Image Generation
36.body: with Diffusion Based Models Source: https://sgvr.kaist.ac.kr/~sungeui/CG/Slides/slides_24/CS380_Talk3_Diffusion.pdf Diffusion Process Diffusion models are inspired by non-equilibrium thermodynamics. For small fraction of the time, it is difficult to determine whether particles are moving in the direction of mixing or in the opposite direction.

37.title: Text to Image Generation
37.body: with Diffusion Based Models Source: https://sgvr.kaist.ac.kr/~sungeui/CG/Slides/slides_24/CS380_Talk3_Diffusion.pdf Diffusion Process If we look at the movement of single molecule on very short time scale, it follows Gaussian distribution. Since the direction of mixing and the opposite direction are the same in very short time, the opposite direction also follows Gaussian distribution.

38.title: Text to Image Generation
38.body: with Diffusion Based Models Source: https://sgvr.kaist.ac.kr/~sungeui/CG/Slides/slides_24/CS380_Talk3_Diffusion.pdf Diffusion Process Just as we viewed the molecule motion as Gaussian-distributed noise We add Gaussian-distributed noise to the pixel

39.title: Denoising Diffusion Models
39.body: Source: https://sgvr.kaist.ac.kr/~sungeui/CG/Slides/slides_24/CS380_Talk3_Diffusion.pdf Denoising diffusion models consist of two processes: Forward diffusion process that gradually adds noise to input Reverse denoising process that learns to generate data by denoising

42.title: Denoising Diffusion Models
42.body: Source: https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2021-07-11-diffusion-models/ Forward diffusion process: Introducing noise In the forward diffusion process, the small Gaussian noise is incrementally added to the distribution over steps, resulting in series of increasingly noisy samples. mean variance 2

43.title: Denoising Diffusion Models
43.body: Source: https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2021-07-11-diffusion-models/ Reverse denoising process: Removing noise We can directly calculate (xt1xt) because it involves complex data-related calculations. Instead, we model the reverse process with neural network to estimate (xt1xt). Model

47.title: Multimodal Embedding Spaces
47.body: Multimodal embedding spaces are typically learned by collecting large amounts of paired multimodal data from the Internet, often images and text, and learning encoders for each modality using pairwise similarity objective.

48.title: Multimodal Embedding Spaces
48.body: Multimodal learning The multi-modal nature of MLLMs is powered by two encoder models (Text encoder and Image encoder) trained to speak the same language.

49.title: Multimodal Embedding Spaces
49.body: Source: 4M-21: An Any-to-Any Vision Model for Tens of Tasks and Modalities, NeurIPS, 2024. Multimodal learning: Generalization across any-to-any modalities [NeurIPS 2024]

50.title: Observations [Google Research 2023]
50.body: Open-web datasets used to train open text-to-image models suffer from significan't issues. To be specific, the captions in the LAION dataset, used to train vision-language models, come from alt HTML tags (Alttext) and often describes only narrow aspect of the image, neglecting significan't visual details. For example, an image of person can have as Alttext the name of the person and the name of the photographer, but not description of their appearance, their clothes, their position, or the background. Source: Picture is Worth Thousand Words: Principled Recaptioning Improves Image Generation, arXiv, 2023.

53.title: The RECAP Framework
53.body: This work proposes to relabel the corpus with improved captions that are auto-generated by custom I2T (image-to-text) model, so as to increase sample efficiency and allow vision-language model to better understand the relations between captions and images.

72.title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach
72.body: Themselves to Use Tools [NeurIPS 2023] Key steps: Given an input text, we first sample position and corresponding API call candidates c1i, c2i,., cki. We then execute these API calls and filter out all calls which do not reduce the loss Li over the next tokens. All remaining API calls are interleaved with the original text, resulting in new text.

75.title: Memory
75.body: Append-only Limited context Limited attention Do not persist over new tasks Stores experience, skills, knowledge. Persist over new experience

76.title: Transforming Experience into Long-Term Memory
76.body: Humans learn from prior mistakes to perform better on task. Self-reflection is mechanism to build long-term memory of self-reflective experiences which allows humans to identify their own errors and self-suggest lessons to learn from their mistakes over time.

77.title: Transforming Experience into Long-Term Memory
77.body: For agents, however, generating useful reflective feedback is challenging since it requires: good understanding of where the model made mistakes; the ability to generate summary containing actionable insights for improvement.

78.title: Reflexion [NeurIPS 2023]
78.body: Reflexion uses verbal reinforcement to help agents learn from prior failings,., converting binary (., success/fail) or scalar feedback from the environment into verbal feedback in the form of textual summary, which is then added as additional context for the MLLM agent in the next episode. Reflexion utilizes three distinct models: Actor: generating text and actions Evaluator: scoring the outputs produced by the Actor Self-Reflection model: generating verbal reinforcement cues to assist the Actor in self-improvement. Source: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning, NeurIPS, 2023.

79.title: The Reflexion process
79.body: Initialization The Actor produces trajectory " by interacting with the environment. The Evaluator then produces score r" which is computed as #=M$("), where # is only scalar reward for trial t. The Self-Reflection model analyzes the set of {", r"} to produce summary sr" which is stored in the memory mem, where sr# is verbal experience feedback for trial t. Repeat The Actor, Evaluator, and Self-Reflection model work together through trials in loop until the Evaluator deems sr# to be correct. Note that after each trial t, sr#, is appended mem.

103.title: Knowledge
103.body: Wen-Huang Cheng National Taiwan University wenhuang@csie.ntu.edu.tw Artificial Intelligence

110.title: This Lecture – Agenda
110.body: Multimodal Knowledge Implicit Knowledge Multimodal Learning Modality Missing Explicit Knowledge Multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation (mRAG) Case Study: Dynamic Visual Question Answering (Dyn-VQA) Classic Logic-Based Agents

112.title: Multimodal Knowledge in AI Agents
112.body: The integration of multimodal understanding is essential for developing sophisticated generalist AI agents. In the context of AI agents, knowledge refers to the processing and representation of diverse data types (such as text, images, and speech) within unified MLLM framework.

113.title: Multimodal Knowledge in AI Agents
113.body: Two types of knowledge: implicit and explicit Implicit knowledge is typically what large-scale language models encapsulate after being trained on vast amounts of text data. Explicit knowledge is structured and can be directly queried, such as the information found in knowledge bases or databases, which was traditionally used to enhance AI reasoning capabilities by referencing verifiable external resources.

114.title: Implicit knowledge: Mixed-Modal Pre-Training
114.body: two multimodal paradigm MLLM Visual Question Answering: What vehicles are these?: Yellow taxi

116.title: Implicit knowledge: Mixed-Modal Pre-Training
116.body: However, training early-fusion MLLMs is challenging: Demanding significan'tly larger datasets and computational resources compared to single-modality models Each modality introduces unique optimization challenges, often exhibiting conflicting training dynamics in dense transformer model

117.title: The two main challenges
117.body: Demanding larger datasets and computations For example, Chameleon (released by Meta, 2024) is trained on 9.2 trillion training tokens (including image tokens) to match LLaMA2, which is trained on 2 trillion training tokens for text performance. Inherent differences in how different modalities are processed In the feature space (across layers), PCA results show the distinct clustering by modality (text, speech, image).

118.title: An alternative solution (by Meta, 2024):
118.body: Mixture-of-Transformers Architecture (MoT) Modality-Specific Parameter Decoupling Each modality employs separate set of non-embedding transformer parameters including the feedforward network, attention matrices and layer normalization. During training, each modality can be supervised using modality-specific losses.

119.title: Mixture-of-Transformers
119.body: Architecture (MoT) The proposed MoT decouples the parameters by modality while maintaining global self-attention.

120.title: Experiments
120.body: Three settings (1) Multi-modal (Text+Image) experiment setting with autoregressive objectives (2) Extended multi-modal experiment with speech modality (Text+Image+Speech) (3) Multi-modal experiment with modality-specific objectives

121.title: Experiments: Setting (1)
121.body: MoT (pre-training at 7B scale) is compared against two baselines: dense transformer model Mixture-of-Experts model with 4 experts (MoE-4x) This plots the training steps required by MoT and MoE-4x to reach equivalent loss values as the dense model.

122.title: What is a Mixture of Experts (MoE)?
122.body: Background The scale of model is one of the most important axes for better model quality. Given fixed computing budget, training larger model for fewer steps is better than training smaller model for more steps. Mixture of Experts enable models to be pretrained with far less compute, which means you can dramatically scale up the model or dataset size with the same compute budget as dense model. In particular, MoE model should achieve the same quality as its dense counterpart much faster during pretraining. Source: https://huggingface.co/blog/moe

123.title: What is a Mixture of Experts (MoE)?
123.body: Architecture MoE consists of two main elements: Sparse MoE layers are used instead of dense feed-forward network (FFN) layers. MoE layers have certain number of experts (. 8), where each expert is neural network. gate network or router, that determines which tokens are sent to which expert. Source: https://huggingface.co/blog/moe

140.body: Disentangled Representation Learning (DRL) aims to learn model capable of identifying and disentangling the underlying factors hidden in the observable data. DRL for Facial Expression Recognition (FER) Related Technique: Disentangled Representation Learning (DRL)

141.body: Facial Chirality Psychological studies show that emotional faces are asymmetric. Powell,.Ryan, and James.Schirillo. "Asymmetrical facial expressions in portraits and hemispheric laterality: literature review." Laterality 14.6 (2009): 545-572. However, face image and its reflection are with exact the same expression. Effective Feature Extraction for FER Ling Lo, HongXia Xie, Hong-Han Shuai and Wen-Huang Cheng, Facial Chirality: Using Self-Face Reflection to Learn Discriminative Features for Facial Expression Recognition, ICME 2021 (Best Paper Award)

142.body: Our idea: decoupling model based on facial chirality to extract facial features for facial expression recognition. 145.title: Explicit knowledge
145.body: Let consider one task for now: question answering (QA).: What is the last day of classes for the next semester at National Taiwan University?: What is 1 + 2?: 3 Requires external knowledge

146.title: Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
146.body: for Knowledge Answer knowledge-intensive questions with Extra corpora retriever

147.title: VQA (Visual Question Answering)
147.body: VQA is task that involves answering questions about an image. The goal of VQA is to teach machines to understand the content of an image and answer questions about it in natural language. image question Which one of them has the highest GDP?

148.title: Knowledge-based VQA
148.body: Knowledge-based VQA takes three different information sources to predict the answer: input visual information (image), input question, and the external knowledge. Source: KAT: Knowledge Augmented Transformer for Vision-and-Language, NAACL, 2022.

149.title: mRAG
149.body: (Multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation) mRAG aims to provide MLLMs with more comprehensive, accurate and up-to-date world knowledge from external source. Two categories of mRAG based on the retrieval method (1) One category employs visual encoding model for direct image representation, and then retrieves the knowledge from knowledge base with the highest feature similarity.

150.title: Example: KAT [NAACL 2022]
150.body: Sliding Window External Source (., Wikipedia) Knowledge (., Entity: Description) Trainera: trainera is traditional boat of the. Answering Model Answer: couch Source: KAT: Knowledge Augmented Transformer for Vision-and-Language, NAACL, 2022.

151.title: REVIVE [NeurIPS 2022]:
151.body: Regional Visual Representation Source: REVIVE: Regional Visual Representation Matters in Knowledge-Based Visual Question Answering, NeurIPS, 2022.

152.title: mRAG
152.body: (Multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation) mRAG aims to provide MLLMs with more comprehensive, accurate and up-to-date world knowledge from external source. Two categories of mRAG based on the retrieval method (1) One category employs visual encoding model for direct image representation, and then retrieves the knowledge from knowledge base with the highest feature similarity. (2) The other category first transforms the input image into textual representation utilizing off-the-shelf tools and then performs text retrieval.

153.title: mRAG
153.body: (Multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation) mRAG aims to provide MLLMs with more comprehensive, accurate and up-to-date world knowledge from external source. Which one of them has the highest GDP? South Korea Example: mRAG based VQA (Visual Question Answering)

155.title: RA-VQA-v2 [NeurIPS 2023]
155.body: The answer generator takes the text query, the image, and the retrieved documents as input, generating one candidate answer per retrieved document. The answer with the highest joint probability is selected. Relevance score between the query and the document is computed by aggregating the fine-grained relevance at token level with late interaction mechanism.

156.title: RA-VQA-v2 [NeurIPS 2023]
156.body: The relevance score between question-image pair!q= (q, I) and document d= (d, I!) is computed as follows: r"q, d= r q, I, d, I!=) "#$ %(' max*#$ %('!)"q" d*.!q l(!q) (with tokens) d l(d) (with tokens) MaxSim!q!

157.title: Challenges with mRAG
157.body: Heuristic mRAG methods typically predefined fixed retrieval processes, suffering from challenges: (1) Non-adaptive Retrieval Queries Referring to the fixed retrieval processes and query structures of heuristic mRAG methods (2) Overloaded Retrieval Queries Referring to heuristic mRAG methods merely format single query by concatenating textual descriptions of objects in images with input questions

158.title: Dynamic questions
158.body: Dynamic questions are defined as questions that require the model to flexibly provide knowledge retrieval solutions, where the query, tool, and time of retrievals are all variable. These questions cannot be solved by predefined retrieval process. Example types of dynamic questions: (1) Questions with rapidly changing answers (2) Questions requiring multi-modal knowledge (3) Multi-hop questions

159.title: Dynamic questions
159.body: Since the context knowledge of such question updates frequently, the retrieved content may be mixed with outdated and newer knowledge that is difficult to distinguish. This requires mARG methods to flexibly plan additional retrievals based on feedback from current retrieved content for further comprehension, rather than merely one-time retrieval.

162.title: OmniSearch [ICLR 2025]
162.body: OmniSearch: self-adaptive planning agent for multimodal retrieval The underlying idea is to emulate the human behavior in question solution which dynamically decomposes complex multimodal questions into sub-question chains with retrieval action. At each step, OmniSearch flexibly adjusts the next action according to question-solving state and retrieved content, with diverse purposes such as deepening comprehension of retrieved content, modifying retrieval method for current sub-question, proposing the next sub-question, etc. Source: Benchmarking Multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation with Dynamic VQA Dataset and Self-adaptive Planning Agent, ICLR, 2025.

163.title: OmniSearch
163.body: Which one of them has the highest GDP? South Korea Taiwan

164.title: The OmniSearch framework
164.body: The planning agent is the core module that formulates sub-questions and plans the subsequent retrieval action based on real-world feedback (., retrieved content or solver output). Actual retrieval action is execute by the retriever. Then, sub-question solver generates feedback on sub-question based on the retrieval content and update it to the planner.

165.title: The Dyn-VQA dataset
165.body: Comparison of knowledge-seeking VQA datasets The annotation of fast or slow is determined by whether the updating occurs on yearly basis.

166.title: The prompt used in original MLLMs, heuristic mRAG-empowered MLLMs, and the OmniSearch

173.title: Some of the following slides credit to:
173.body: Harvard University-CS 50: Artificial Intelligence in Python

174.title: Classic logic-based agents
174.body: agents that reason by operating on logic-based representations of knowledge

175.title: An example
175.body: Consider the following sentences (1) If it didn' rain, Amy visited Bobby today. (2) Amy visited Bobby or Carson today, but not both. (3) Amy visited Carson today. Can we answer the question Did it rain today? Amy did not visit Bobby today. It rained today.

177.title: sentence
177.body: an assertion about the world in knowledge representation language

180.title: Logical
180.body: Connectives not U and U or implication biconditional

185.title: Implication represent a structure of “if P then Q”
185.body: For example: if: It is raining and: indoors then means If it is raining, then indoors. In the case of implies, is called the antecedent and is called the consequent.

186.title: When the antecedent is true, the whole implication is true in
186.body: the case that the consequent is true., if it is raining and indoors, then the sentence if it is raining, then indoors is true. When the antecedent is true, the implication is false if the consequent is false., if outside while it is raining, then the sentence If it is raining, then indoors is false.

187.title: However, when the antecedent is false, the implication is
187.body: always true, regardless of the consequent. Logically, we can learn anything from an implication if the antecedent is false. Looking at our example, if it is not raining, the implication doesn say anything about whether indoors or not. could be an indoors type and never walk outside, even when it is not raining, or could be an outdoors type and be outside all the time when it is not raining. When the antecedent is false, we say that the implication is trivially true.

189.title: model
189.body: assignment of truth value to every propositional symbol ("possible world") The model is the truth-value assignment that provides information about the world

190.title: A model
190.body: : It is raining.: It is Tuesday. {=true,=false} In fact, there are more possible models, say 22=4 because we have two propositions.

191.title: knowledge base
191.body: set of sentences known by knowledge-based agent

192.title: Entailment ()
192.body: if (entails) then, in every model where sentence is true, sentence is also true.

193.title: For example, if α: “It is a Tuesday in January” and β: “It
193.body: is January, then we know that.If it is true that it is Tuesday in January, we also know that it is January. Entailment is different from implication. Implication is logical connective between two propositions. Entailment, on the other hand, is relation that means that if all the information in is true, then all the information in is true.

194.title: inference
194.body: the process of deriving new sentences from old ones

195.title: ! If it didn't rain, Amy visited Bobby today.
195.body: " Amy visited Bobby or Carson today, but not both. # Amy visited Carson today. $ Amy did not visit Bobby today. % It rained today. (4)(5) are inferred from (1)(2)(3).

197.title: Model Checking
197.body: way to infer new knowledge based on existing knowledge

198.title: Model Checking
198.body: To determine if KB (answering the question: can we conclude that is true based on our knowledge base): Enumerate all possible models. If in every model where KB is true, then KB entails.Otherwise, KB does not entail.

199.title: P: It is a Tuesday.
199.body: : It is raining. KB: (U) (is true): Harry will go for run. (is false) Query: KB First, we enumerate all possible models.

200.title: P
200.body: KB Next, we go through every model and check whether it is true given our knowledge base. KB: (U)

201.title: P
201.body: KB Next, we go through every model and check whether it is true given our knowledge base. KB: (U)

202.title: P
202.body: KB Next, we go through every model and check whether it is true given our knowledge base. KB: (U) Due to being in the KB, we know that in the case where is true and is false, must be true. Thus, we say that our KB is false for the model where is false and true for the model where is true. (U)

203.title: P
203.body: KB Looking at this table, there is only one model where our knowledge base is true. In this model, we see that is also true. By the definition of entailment, if is true in all models where the KB is true, then KB.

204.title: A Python Code Example
204.body: Let look at how knowledge and logic can be represented as code. from logic import * # Create new classes, each having name, or symbol, representing each proposition. rain=Symbol("rain") # It is raining. bobby=Symbol(bobby") # Amy visited Bobby carson=Symbol(carson") # Amy visited Carson # Save sentences into the KB knowledge=And(# Starting from the "And" logical connective, because each proposition represents knowledge that we know to be true. Implication(Not(rain), bobby), # (It is raining) (Amy visited Bobby) Or(bobby, carson), # (Amy visited Bobby) (Amy visited Carson). Not(And(bobby, carson)), # (Amy visited Bobby Amy visited Carson). Amy did not visit both Bobby and Carson. carson # Amy visited Carson. Note that while previous propositions contained multiple symbols with connectors, this is proposition consisting of one symbol. This means that we take as fact that, in this KB, Amy visited Carson.)

206.title: Knowledge Engineering
206.body: the process of figuring out how to represent propositions and logic in AI

207.title: Clue
207.body: In the game, murder was committed by person using tool in location. People, tools, locations are represented by cards.

208.title: Clue
208.body: People Rooms Weapons Col. Mustard Ballroom Knife Prof. Plum Kitchen Revolver Ms. Scarlet Library Wrench

209.title: People
209.body: Rooms Weapons One card of each category is picked at random and put it in an envelope, and it is up to the participants to uncover whodunnit.

211.title: Clue
211.body: Propositional Symbols mustard plum scarlet ballroom kitchen library knife revolver wrench

212.title: Clue
212.body: (mustard U plum U scarlet) (ballroom U kitchen U library) (knife U revolver U wrench) plum mustard U library U revolver We can start creating our knowledge base by adding the rules of the same. (hints obtained during the course of the game)

213.title: Mastermind
213.body: Another type of puzzle that can be solved using propositional logic is Mastermind game. In this game, player one arranges colors in certain order, and then player two has to guess this order. Each turn, player two makes guess, and player one gives back number, indicating how many colors player two got right. Let simulate game with four colors.

214.title: Remarks
214.body: Real-world knowledge plays vital role in human intelligence. Dynamics Physical World Knowledge Taxonomy Horizon Deep Semantics (slow-thinking) Surface Semantics (fast-thinking) Static Dynamic Object Permanence Object Properties Spatial Relation Object Affordance Temporal Relation Planning Situation/Event (Image) Action (Video)
